title_ar: الصحابة: الجيل الأول في سير أعلام النبلاء
content_en:
The Siyar A'lam an-Nubala opens with the generation that adh-Dhahabi considered the most noble in human history after the prophets: the Companions of the Prophet Muhammad. The biographical entries for the major Companions are among the longest in the entire work, reflecting adh-Dhahabi's conviction that their status was unique and that their lives deserved the fullest treatment he could provide. At the same time, his approach to Companion biography is not hagiographic — he brings the same critical eye to their accounts that he brings to every other generation.

The entry for Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, the first caliph, establishes the pattern adh-Dhahabi will follow for major Companions. He records the biographical essentials — birth, early life, relationship with the Prophet, contributions to Islam, death — drawing on the full range of historical and hadith sources available to him. He notes the narrations about Abu Bakr's preeminence, assesses their authenticity, and integrates them into a portrait that is both historically grounded and spiritually reverential. His conclusion that Abu Bakr was the most excellent of the Companions after the prophets is stated clearly and supported with evidence.

For Umar ibn al-Khattab, Uthman ibn Affan, and Ali ibn Abi Talib — the other three of the Rightly Guided Caliphs — adh-Dhahabi navigates territory made sensitive by the history of sectarian conflict within Islam. The disputes over the caliphate that divided the early community did not disappear with the passage of centuries; they continued to shape how each group of Muslims understood these figures. Adh-Dhahabi's position is that of mainstream Sunni tradition: all four caliphs are highly honored, their rank follows a specific order established by scholarly consensus, and the disputes that occurred among the Companions after the Prophet's death are understood with charity toward all parties.

His treatment of Ali ibn Abi Talib is particularly careful, given that Ali's biography is the most contested among the Companions. Adh-Dhahabi presents Ali's extraordinary learning, spiritual rank, and closeness to the Prophet in terms that affirm his honored status in Sunni Islam, while also discussing the historical events — the Battle of Jamal, the Battle of Siffin, the arbitration — with a historian's attention to evidence rather than a partisan's desire to assign blame.

Beyond the four caliphs, the Siyar covers the broader circle of major Companions: the ten given glad tidings of Paradise, the Emigrants and the Helpers, the great hadith transmitters, and figures known primarily from specific incidents or narrations. Adh-Dhahabi's scholarly priorities shape which Companions receive the fullest treatment — those who were major transmitters of hadith or who made significant contributions to the preservation of knowledge receive more attention than those known primarily for their military service or political roles.

The Companion entries also function as a demonstration of adh-Dhahabi's broader historical method. He weighs contradictory reports about the same figure, notes when a source is unreliable, and indicates where later elaboration has distorted an earlier, simpler account. This critical approach, applied even to the most venerated generation, reflects his conviction that honest scholarship serves the tradition better than uncritical reverence.
